title: Harnessing Human Expertise
full_name: Harnessing Human Expertise for High-Precision Robotic Assembly in Industrialized Construction: A Sample-Efficient Installer-in-the-Loop Interactive Reinforcement Learning Framework
A2_action_abstraction: Low-level chunked end-effector delta-pose plus binary vacuum command
A2_observation_type: High-dim multimodal dual-RGB observation (wrist+side 128x128x3) fused with low-dim 18D proprioception (joint states and end-effector pose)
_alias_note: Installer-in-the-loop offline-to-online RL for tolerance-critical window seating with 2 mm per-side clearance in MuJoCo; policy observes dual-view RGB and proprioception and outputs fixed-horizon action chunks (h=10) of 7D controls (6D incremental end-effector pose plus binary vacuum), tracked by an OSC controller. Learning pipeline has three stages: offline demonstration pretraining, non-updating warm-start value calibration, and online fine-tuning with event-driven binary human takeovers at high-risk contact boundaries (binding/wedging/jamming). RL backbone uses chunk-level Q-learning with a flow-based multimodal sequence prior (QC-FQL style teacher-student distillation) under sparse terminal acceptance-aligned reward; replay mixes offline demos and online interactions in matched ratio. Reported simulator stress tests show strong seated success under bounded pose/friction randomization with low cumulative takeover time, but evidence remains simulation-only and explicitly notes no formal deployment safety guarantees.

The trained policy is then validated on a lab-scale mock-up hoist platform synchronized with real-site call datasets using a hardware stack (remote hall-call pagers sensors auto-controller and group controller), showing total transportation-time reductions versus a human-operator model and SCAN/GA/LQF baselines. title: RL Path Planning Mobile Cranes
full_name: Reinforcement Learning-Enhanced Path Planning for Mobile Cranes in Dynamic Construction Environments: A Virtual Reality Simulation Approach
A2_action_abstraction: Mid-level crane motion command policy over tracks/boom/hook DoFs in simulator
A2_observation_type: Low-dim / structured simulator state with crane and payload pose-velocity-rotation plus sensor-derived collision tags and a payload-connected boolean
_alias_note: VR+RL lift-path planning framework for a crawler mobile crane in a 3D simulated construction site. The agent controls crane components through simplified DoFs: tracks (translation and yaw), boom (rotations), and hook vertical motion; actions are penalized by time/energy proxies (track moves highest penalty, then boom, then hook) while rewards are given for successful pickup and final set-down. State includes element locations, velocities, and local rotations for crane/payload/environment plus sensor-based collision information and a binary indicator of whether payload is attached to the hook. Objective jointly targets reduced lifting time, lower energy-related movement cost, and improved collision avoidance. Training over millions of episodes shows convergence to shorter simulation time and higher cumulative rewards. Reported implementation is proof-of-concept and remains simulation-only; notably, obstacle rigid-body collisions were simplified/removed in the presented experiment to ensure full-run training, so real-site dynamic-obstacle robustness remains unvalidated.

title: A New Concept for Large Additive Manufacturing
full_name: A New Concept for Large Additive Manufacturing in Construction: Tower Crane-Based {3D} Printing Controlled by Deep Reinforcement Learning
A2_action_abstraction: Low-level continuous actuator control (jib angular increment u_a_t and aero-pendulum thrust force F_t)
A2_observation_type: Low-dim structured continuous state vector (relative perpendicular extruder-trolley p_t p_dot p_ddot; previous angular increment u_a_prev; thrust F_t; trolley angle u_t; trolley global x y plus velocities/accelerations x_dot y_dot x_ddot y_ddot)
_alias_note: TD3 actor-critic continuous control for TC-based 3D printing using Simscape Multibody; action is {u_a_t, F_t} with bounded ranges (u_a_t in [0.005..0.2] and F_t in [-300..300] N); observation is a structured vector with relative extruder-trolley perpendicular dynamics plus trolley global pose and derivatives (p_t p_dot p_ddot u_a_t F_t u_t x y x_dot y_dot x_ddot y_ddot); simulated discrete-time MDP with dt = 0.1 s and horizon T = 60 s; reward encourages fast trajectory following while suppressing swing: r_t = u_a_prev * 25/(1+1e8 p_t); training uses minibatch 512 and replay buffer 1e6 (actor lr 1e-4 critic lr 1e-3 discount 0.99) with roughly 5000 simulation episodes; evaluated against an uncontrolled case under externally applied disturbance forces on the extruder (d(t)=A*sin(f*t+phi)+b with A in [-200..200] N and f in [6.28..62.8] rad/s and phi in [0..0.35] rad and b in [0..30] N); controlled case achieves Eurocode 6 EN 1996-2 tolerances with max abs error about 0.08 m and mean abs error about 0.045 m (uncontrolled abs error up to about 0.99 m).

title: Generalized Framework for Wheel Loader Automatic Shoveling Task
full_name: Generalized Framework for Wheel Loader Automatic Shoveling Task with Expert Initialized Reinforcement Learning
A2_action_abstraction: Low-level continuous joint position targets (boom and bucket trajectory setpoints)
A2_observation_type: Low-dim / structured joint state and target-distance (theta_boom theta_bucket theta_dot_boom theta_dot_bucket dr)
_alias_note: CoL (Circle of Learning) expert-initialized TD3 for fast wheel-loader bucket shoveling; actor outputs continuous boom and bucket position targets while an inverse-dynamics controller tracks them using estimated dynamics B(q) qdd + C(q,q_dot) q_dot + tau_f(q_dot) + g; hydraulic dead-zones are cut from valve-command data and remaining non-linear effects are modeled as Coulomb + viscous friction; states are [theta_boom theta_bucket theta_dot_boom theta_dot_bucket dr] where dr is relative distance from bucket tip to pile edge measured by GNSS and joint kinematics measured by IMUs; reward is zero until bucket penetration then combines a completion term lambda1(1-tanh(d_end)) and a fill-rate term lambda2(V/Vmax) evaluated only at the last two steps of each 100-step episode (dt=0.01s); simulation uses NVIDIA Isaac Gym (PhysX) with cube sand particles and domain randomization on observation noise mu(0,0.002) and pile friction in [0.2,0.9]; pretraining uses 10 expert demonstrations with behavior-cloning loss LBC plus L2 regularization then switches to off-policy CoL/TD3 updates from replay; training takes about 2 hours on CPU and reaches avg fill rate about 89.8% vs TD3 about 20.6% within the same duration (expert about 80%); real experiments on MUSTANG 2040 validate sim-2-real with wet soil avg fill rate about 95% and medium coarse gravel avg about 88% (two safety terminations) and show close boom and bucket trajectory matching, with remaining trajectory roughness attributed to uncertain bucket-media interactions (pressure sensing or better interaction models suggested).

title: Adaptive Excavation Automation Complex Soil
full_name: Adaptive Excavation Automation in Complex Soil Environments Using Reinforcement Learning
A2_observation_type: Low-dim / structured soil-parameter and excavation-state representation
_alias_note: paper-level check: PPO state uses FEE soil parameters and grid/height summary variables rather than raw high-dimensional perception.

title: Large-Scale Robotic Material Handling
full_name: Large-Scale Robotic Material Handling: Learning, Planning, and Control
A2_observation_type: Low-dim / engineered 2.5D soil heightmap (hsoil) for attack-point planning + proprioceptive joint state and action/velocity history for waypoint-following/throwing (tube-distance and inferred binary gripper open/close)
_alias_note: Full framework for autonomous bulk material handling with grasp-and-dump cycles on a fixed-base 40 t hydraulic material handler. RL modules: (1) attack-point planner (PPO) outputs a 3D grasp attack point to maximize scooped volume / minimize number of cycles, operating on an engineered heightmap hsoil derived from filtered pile geometry; policy input is flattened hsoil (optionally bicubic-interpolated to coarser grids) with observation-noise models (sensor Gaussian noise, LiDAR dropout/spikes simulated during training) and domain-randomized pile initializations. (2) waypoint-following controller (PPO) tracks provided waypoint sequences with bounded deviation via tube constraints and minimizes oscillations of a free-swinging underactuated clamshell gripper; action includes direct slew joystick command plus boom/stick velocity references, constrained by actuator-range clipping and using a history window of actions/velocities to infer latent dynamics. (3) throwing controller extends waypoint-following by adding a binary gripper open action (with simulated release delay) and compensating for granular release by training on discrete sequential rigid loads L1–L3 (parabolic trajectories with tool-oscillation-dependent spread), rewarding accurate final throw while continuing tube/safety terms. Low-level control: boom/stick track velocity references via PI + LUT feedforward; slew dynamics are learned via a hybrid data-driven pressure-to-velocity model (two-stage MLPs) integrated into simulation; gripper oscillation modeled as a double-pendulum with dissipative term. Simulation/training: RaiSim with 512 parallel environments (sim 50 Hz, control 10 Hz), 15 s episodes, multiple PPO updates and history-dependent observations; domain randomization includes hydraulic delay/friction/inertia and initial joint configurations. Safety & execution: obstacle-aware arm path planning uses OctoMap + RRT* and updates every phase change inside a high-level state machine (WAYPOINT/GRASP/THROW), with additional joint-limit safety layer and STOP on failure; RL controllers enforce bounded tracking within the tube for safe motion in cluttered workspaces. Evidence: real-world experiments validate two tasks (bulk pile management and dump-truck loading), comparing against human operators for precision/repeatability/safety; residual sim-to-real errors mainly attributed to slew/model mismatch and uncontrolled granular free-fall after release.

title: Eulerian Circuit-Based RL Path Optimization
full_name: A Eulerian Circuit-Based Reinforcement Learning Path Optimization Method for {3D} Concrete Printing
A2_action_abstraction: Low-level discrete next-edge selection along the Eulerian graph (Q-learning at branching points)
A2_observation_type: Low-dim / engineered discrete traversal state (current point index among 15600; branching/non-branching + forward-only constraint)
_alias_note: 3D concrete printing slice is treated as graph traversal: 15600 discretized points form the RL environment state; 32 branching points are the decision vertices while non-branching points are visited once and enforce forward-only motion (no reverse). Fleury algorithm first generates multiple Eulerian circuits from the same start vertex; RL (tabular Q-learning) optimizes which next-edge/next branching choice to follow along those circuits. Reward is distributed to guide global behavior: positive reward at branching states with higher value rb<=re when the agent follows the Eulerian circuit order; turning penalty Rt(s,a) applies negative reward when the printhead changes direction/action (reducing turning and lift/start-stop). Training uses epsilon-greedy with linearly decayed epsilon from 0.3 to 0.05 and terminates after exhaustive traversal with no redundancy (unvisited list becomes empty). Reported outcome for N=13: non-print ratio becomes zero (one-stroke Eulerian traversal), turning times reduced by 20.2% vs Eulerian circuit baseline; lifting times reduced by 59.6% vs ZigZag and by 36.3% vs Eulerian. Gains increase as geometry complexity grows; paths exhibit regular sub-path structure beneficial for subsequent G-code digitization/standardization.

The controller jointly actuates slew and arm to reach Cartesian targets while damping tool oscillations, and is validated on a real approximately 40 t prototype machine with comparisons to operators. Evidence supports strong real-machine control performance in a construction-adjacent material-handling setting, without an independent runtime veto/fallback layer.

title: Fixed-Time Convergence Adaptive Robust Crane
full_name: Fixed-Time Convergence Ding2025Adaptive Robust Controller for Overhead Cranes Based on Reinforcement Learning Considering Input Saturation
A2_action_abstraction: Low-level continuous actuator-force control (trolley driving force u1 and lifting force u2 under saturation constraints)

Control objective is trolley positioning and sway suppression with rope-length regulation under matched/unmatched disturbances and actuator input saturation. Validation is simulation-only (multiple disturbance/saturation ablations and baseline comparisons, including PDSMC/FNFTSMC/FINFTSMC), so assurance is controller-internal robust design rather than an independent runtime safety monitor.

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning-based Task Assignment and Path Planning
full_name: Deep Reinforcement Learning-based Task Assignment and Path Planning for Multi-agent Construction Robots
A2_action_abstraction: Low-level 1D linear velocity commands per robot toward assigned goals
A2_observation_type: High-dim observation using 2D LiDAR range scans plus relative robot-goal/robot states and idle-time scalars
_alias_note: MAPPO proof-of-concept combines MATA (task allocation) and MAPP (multi-agent path planning) for a bricklaying setting using centralized learning with decentralized execution; each robot observes distance-to-assigned-goal plus multi-robot coordination features including idle-time and 2D LiDAR/laser obstacle range measurements; the action is a 1D vector representing linear velocity v_l^t along the robot heading toward its assigned goal; the episodic POMDP terminates on collision or when all robots reach goals or time limit; reward blends task-logic compliance and idle-time penalties with distance-to-shortest-path terms and obstacle/collision penalties; in the reported proof-of-concept some obstacle/path-planning terms are simplified and collision penalties are effectively ruled out so robot-velocity randomness dominates the residual uncertainty; training converges after several thousand episodes (stabilizes around ~5,000 in the fixed-seed report), demonstrating feasibility in ROS Gazebo but no real-robot validation (EVL L0 simulation-level only).

A patch-based contact-driven motion (PCDM) simulator is used for interaction/training; reported results include high path-planning accuracy and force-prediction success in simulation plus transfer-learning fine-tuning on real-world datasets, but no disclosed live closed-loop site deployment or independent runtime veto/fallback safety layer.
